naturalMeaning: Lowercase in everyday Portuguese unless it starts a sentence; capitalized mostly in formal or official contexts.

examples.text: Você vai viajar no carnaval?
examples.translation: Are you traveling for Carnival?

translation: to do Carnival / to go all in on Carnival
naturalMeaning: The everyday Brazilian verb for actively taking part — going out, dancing in blocos, following the escolas — not just "celebrating" from the sidelines. See the culture notes.

examples.text: Esse ano eu vou pular carnaval em Salvador.
examples.translation: This year I'm doing Carnival in Salvador.

naturalMeaning: A group with its own name, theme, and following that parades through the streets playing music — anyone can join in as it passes.

examples.text: Qual bloco vocês vão seguir hoje?
examples.translation: Which bloco are you all following today?

naturalMeaning: Specifies the free, open-air kind of bloco, as opposed to a private or ticketed event — see the culture notes for how this differs from the sambódromo.

examples.text: O bloco de rua sai da praça às dez da manhã.
examples.translation: The street bloco leaves from the square at 10am.

naturalMeaning: Also means "fantasy" in other contexts, but during Carnival season it just means a costume, no matter how simple.

examples.text: Ainda não comprei minha fantasia.
examples.translation: I still haven't bought my costume.

examples.text: Vocês vão se fantasiar de quê esse ano?
examples.translation: What are you all dressing up as this year?

naturalMeaning: Not a school in the literal sense — a large community organization, tied to a neighborhood, that spends the whole year preparing one themed parade.

examples.text: Ela desfila pela escola de samba desde criança.
examples.translation: She's paraded with the samba school since she was a kid.

examples.text: O desfile começa às nove da noite.
examples.translation: The parade starts at 9pm.

translation: to parade / to march in a parade

examples.text: Quantas pessoas vão desfilar este ano?
examples.translation: How many people are parading this year?

naturalMeaning: Also the everyday word for "drum kit" or "battery" — in a Carnival context it means the hundreds-strong percussion section that drives an escola de samba's parade.

examples.text: A bateria dessa escola tem mais de trezentos músicos.
examples.translation: This school's drum section has more than three hundred musicians.

translation: wing (a costumed group within a parade)
naturalMeaning: An escola de samba's parade is organized into alas — each with its own matching fantasia and role in telling the enredo.

examples.text: Minha tia desfila na ala das baianas.
examples.translation: My aunt parades in the baianas wing.

naturalMeaning: A branded t-shirt or tank top sold in advance — buying one is usually what gets you inside the roped-off area around a trio elétrico or paid bloco.

examples.text: Comprei o abadá do bloco pela internet.
examples.translation: I bought the bloco's abadá online.

naturalMeaning: An elevated, ticketed viewing area with its own bar and bathrooms — the opposite experience from being down in the crowd.

examples.text: Quanto custa a entrada do camarote?
examples.translation: How much is admission to the VIP box?

naturalMeaning: The cheaper, general-seating alternative to a camarote at the sambódromo.

examples.text: Compramos ingresso pra arquibancada.
examples.translation: We bought tickets for the grandstand.

naturalMeaning: Short, catchy, decades-old songs everyone knows the words to — played at street blocos, distinct from the newer samba-enredo written for a specific escola's parade.

examples.text: O bloco só toca marchinha antiga.
examples.translation: The bloco only plays old-school marchinhas.

naturalMeaning: Written fresh each year to match the escola's enredo — everyone in the parade, all several thousand of them, sings it live the whole way down the avenue.

examples.text: O samba-enredo desse ano fala sobre a Amazônia.
examples.translation: This year's theme samba is about the Amazon.

naturalMeaning: The narrative — often historical, folkloric, or political — that an escola's costumes, floats, and samba-enredo are all built around that year.

examples.text: Qual é o enredo da escola este ano?
examples.translation: What's the school's theme this year?

naturalMeaning: The feminine form is foliã. Used for anyone out celebrating, not just costumed performers.

examples.text: Milhares de foliões tomaram a avenida.
examples.translation: Thousands of revelers took over the avenue.

naturalMeaning: A truck built as a rolling stage, blasting live music as it crawls through the crowd — the centerpiece of street Carnival in Salvador, Bahia.

examples.text: O trio elétrico já está descendo a avenida.
examples.translation: The trio elétrico is already coming down the avenue.

naturalMeaning: Fast, danceable, born out of Salvador's Carnival — the genre you'll hear blasting from most trios elétricos.

examples.text: Eu cresci ouvindo axé no carnaval.
examples.translation: I grew up listening to axé at Carnival.

naturalMeaning: Danced with a small umbrella and fast, acrobatic footwork — the signature sound and dance of Carnival in Recife and Olinda.

examples.text: Em Olinda, todo mundo dança frevo na rua.
examples.translation: In Olinda, everyone dances frevo in the street.

naturalMeaning: The rope held up around a trio elétrico or paid bloco, marking off the abadá-only area from everyone else.

examples.text: Só quem tem abadá entra dentro da corda.
examples.translation: Only people with an abadá get inside the cordon.

naturalMeaning: Slang for the free-roaming crowd bouncing around outside a trio elétrico's corda — livelier, cheaper, and more chaotic than being inside.

examples.text: A gente prefere ficar na pipoca, é mais barato.
examples.translation: We prefer to stay in the pipoca, it's cheaper.

naturalMeaning: A purpose-built avenue with grandstands on both sides, where escolas de samba parade and are judged — Rio's, designed by Oscar Niemeyer, is the most famous.

examples.text: O desfile das escolas especiais acontece no sambódromo.
examples.translation: The top-division schools' parade happens at the sambadrome.

naturalMeaning: A featured dancer, often a celebrity, who leads the drum section down the avenue in an elaborate fantasia — a judged, high-visibility role.

examples.text: A rainha de bateria treinou o ano inteiro pra esse momento.
examples.translation: The drum-section queen trained all year for this moment.

translation: master of ceremonies (dance partner to the porta-bandeira)
naturalMeaning: Dances alongside the porta-bandeira, bowing and circling her as they present the escola's flag — one of the most heavily judged pairs in the parade.

examples.text: O mestre-sala e a porta-bandeira abrem a apresentação da escola.
examples.translation: The master of ceremonies and flag bearer open the school's presentation.

naturalMeaning: Carries and spins the escola's flag in an elaborate hoop-skirted fantasia, partnered with the mestre-sala.

examples.text: A porta-bandeira girou a bandeira sem parar de sorrir.
examples.translation: The flag bearer spun the flag without ever stopping smiling.

naturalMeaning: The first group to appear, usually a short choreographed piece that introduces the enredo before the rest of the parade follows.

examples.text: A comissão de frente já entrou na avenida.
examples.translation: The front commission has already entered the avenue.

examples.text: O carro alegórico principal tem quase dez metros de altura.
examples.translation: The main float is almost ten meters tall.

examples.text: Ela passou purpurina no rosto antes de sair.
examples.translation: She put glitter on her face before heading out.

examples.text: A rua ficou cheia de confete.
examples.translation: The street was covered in confetti.

naturalMeaning: Used by a bloco or bateria's puxador (leader) to cue rhythm changes — a sharp signal everyone in the group listens for.

examples.text: Quando o apito toca três vezes, a bateria para.
examples.translation: When the whistle blows three times, the drum section stops.

examples.text: Os dançarinos se arrumam no camarim antes do desfile.
examples.translation: The dancers get ready in the dressing room before the parade.

naturalMeaning: The symbolic, chosen-for-his-girth "king" who receives the city's keys at the opening ceremony, declaring Carnival officially underway.

examples.text: O Rei Momo recebeu a chave da cidade no sábado.
examples.translation: King Momo received the key to the city on Saturday.

naturalMeaning: The illustrated design a fantasia is built from, drawn by the escola's carnavalesco (art director) months in advance.

examples.text: O carnavalesco mostrou o croqui da fantasia principal.
examples.translation: The art director showed the sketch of the main costume.

naturalMeaning: The formal, official term for an escola de samba as a registered community organization — used in rulebooks and news coverage more than in casual talk.

examples.text: A agremiação foi fundada há mais de sessenta anos.
examples.translation: The association was founded more than sixty years ago.

naturalMeaning: Covers both the literal hangover and the broader exhaustion/comedown of days of partying, not just one night's drinking.

examples.text: Hoje é quarta de cinzas e a ressaca de carnaval tá braba.
examples.translation: It's Ash Wednesday and the post-Carnival crash is rough.

examples.text: Bebe água, se hidrata, o sol tá forte.
examples.translation: Drink water, hydrate, the sun is strong.

examples.text: Passa protetor solar antes de sair de casa.
examples.translation: Put on sunscreen before leaving the house.

translation: gathering point (before a bloco moves off)
naturalMeaning: Where a bloco's crowd builds up and warms up before it actually starts parading — the listed "start time" is really the concentração time, not when it moves.

examples.text: A concentração é na praça às oito, mas o bloco só sai às nove.
examples.translation: The gathering point is at the square at eight, but the bloco doesn't move off until nine.

naturalMeaning: The more technical term for the full costume design of an ala or float, as opposed to the everyday word fantasia.

examples.text: O figurino dessa ala pesa mais de dez quilos.
examples.translation: This wing's costume weighs more than ten kilos.

naturalMeaning: The day Carnival officially ends — many cities' rule of thumb is that Carnival runs from Friday to Tuesday, then everything shuts back down on Wednesday.

examples.text: Na quarta-feira de cinzas, a cidade volta ao normal.
examples.translation: On Ash Wednesday, the city goes back to normal.

naturalMeaning: Used to describe the whole glittery, over-the-top spectacle of a sambódromo parade, beyond the literal costume materials.

examples.text: O desfile foi puro plumas e paetês.
examples.translation: The parade was pure feathers and sequins.

naturalMeaning: Said of someone who dances samba naturally and well, as if it's built into their feet rather than learned.

examples.text: Ela tem muito samba no pé.
examples.translation: She's got real samba in her feet.

translation: to throw yourself into the revelry
naturalMeaning: Close in spirit to pular carnaval — fully letting go and joining the party rather than watching from the side.

examples.text: Chega de trabalhar, hoje é dia de cair na folia!
examples.translation: Enough working, today's a day to throw yourself into the party!

naturalMeaning: Sings the samba-enredo live on a sound truck within the parade so the thousands of participants around him can follow along.

examples.text: O puxador cantou o samba-enredo do início ao fim sem errar.
examples.translation: The lead singer sang the theme samba from start to finish without a mistake.

naturalMeaning: The person responsible for an escola's overall creative vision for the year — the enredo, the croquis, the floats — a high-prestige, closely watched role.

examples.text: O carnavalesco trabalha o ano inteiro pensando no próximo desfile.
examples.translation: The art director works all year thinking about the next parade.

examples.text: Onde vocês compraram o ingresso do camarote?
examples.translation: Where did you all buy the VIP-box ticket?

naturalMeaning: The word you'll actually see on flyers for street blocos — most are gratuito, unlike a camarote or the sambódromo grandstands.

examples.text: A entrada no bloco é gratuita.
examples.translation: Admission to the bloco is free.
